steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls after ‘shade’ and before ‘though most sand gets its shade from…’ The two independent clauses need punctuation that (1) ends the first clause cleanly and (2) allows ‘though’ to introduce a properly structured contrast. A colon can introduce a contrasting elaboration, and ‘though’ at the start of the second clause creates a subordinate clause followed by a main clause — a grammatically complete sentence.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘shade, though most’: A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses; this creates a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘shade, though, most’: The comma after ‘though’ splits ‘though’ from its clause, making the sentence ungrammatical. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘shade: though most’: The colon correctly ends the first independent clause. ‘Though most sand gets its shade from quartz and feldspar, deposits of iron oxide… lend the sand a red tint’ is a well-formed sentence with a subordinate clause followed by a main clause. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘shade, though. Most’: The period after ‘though’ cuts off ‘though’ from anything it can contrast, leaving a fragment (‘shade, though’). ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
